attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department accompanied by police after causing a disturbance at a local government office. He has been attempting to file complaints about surveillance devices he believes have been implanted in his teeth by a criminal organisation. He describes an elaborate network of operatives who monitor his thoughts through these devices and broadcast them to television stations. He can provide detailed explanations of how the technology works and names dozens of people he believes are involved in the conspiracy. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears agitated but is not aggressive. His vital signs are within normal limits. He is well-groomed and makes good eye contact. His speech is pressured but coherent. There are no signs of substance intoxication or withdrawal. He adamantly refuses to consider that his beliefs might be false.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes this patient's psychotic symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
This case demonstrates classic features of systematised delusions, which are characterised by an elaborate and internally consistent network of false beliefs. The patient has constructed a complex explanatory system involving surveillance devices, multiple actors, and technological mechanisms.

Systematised delusions are typically more fixed and resistant to change than other types of delusions. They often involve multiple interconnected ideas that the patient has woven together into a coherent (though false) narrative. In this case, the patient can provide detailed explanations about how the technology works and has identified numerous people involved in the supposed conspiracy.

Simple persecutory delusions would involve more isolated false beliefs without the elaborate framework and explanatory system seen here. For example, a patient might simply believe someone is trying to harm them without constructing an extensive narrative about how and why.

Thought broadcasting is indeed present in this case (the belief that one's thoughts are being transmitted to others), but it is part of a larger delusional system rather than occurring in isolation. The primary feature here is the systematic nature of the delusions.

Delusional mood refers to a pre-psychotic state where patients feel that their environment has changed in some inexplicable way, but have not yet developed fixed delusions. This patient has clearly progressed beyond this stage to fixed, systematised delusions.

Referential delusions involve the belief that external events or objects hold special significance for the individual. While some referential elements may be present here (such as the belief about television stations), the predominant feature is the systematic nature of the persecutory delusions.

The presence of systematised delusions often indicates a more severe form of psychotic illness and may suggest a poorer prognosis than simple delusions. These patients typically require more intensive treatment and may be more resistant to antipsychotic medication.
learningPoints: Systematised delusions are complex, elaborate and internally consistent belief systems that often involve multiple interconnected ideas.
Simple delusions are isolated false beliefs without elaborate explanatory frameworks.
The presence of systematised delusions often indicates a more chronic course and poorer prognosis than simple delusions.
references: American Psychological Association. Systematized delusion (APA Dictionary of Psychology). Washington, DC: APA; n.d. Available at: https://dictionary.apa.org/systematized-delusion

Bortolotti L, Miyazono K, Westra EAJ, editors. Delusions in context. Cham: Palgrave Macmillan; 2018. Open-access edition. Available at: https://library.oapen.org/bitstream/handle/20.500.12657/28276/1/2018_Book_DelusionsInContext.pdf

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
